summary: Drew DeVault's May 2019 status update highlights progress across his open source projects, with email-related developments taking center stage. The major announcement is web-based patch review tools for lists.sr.ht that bridge email-based and web-based code review workflows. He also covers aerc2 gaining email composition, sway 1.1 release candidates, a new Wayland protocol book in progress, and educational git websites. The update reflects a period of high productivity enabled by full-time open source work funded by supporters.
key_points: Web-based patch review tools for lists.sr.ht convert organic email discussions into a GitHub/Gerrit-style review UI without requiring participants to change their workflow
aerc2 now supports composing emails with embedded terminal editor integration, a feature absent even in aerc1
Sway 1.1 is nearing release with idle inhibition and touch support, while DeVault works on Wayland protocol governance and a comprehensive Wayland book
New educational sites git-rebase.io follows git-send-email.io, teaching git primitives rather than just recipes
Wio, a novel Wayland compositor inspired by Plan 9's Rio, was announced as a side project
money_quotes: This approach allows users who prefer a web-based workflow to interact with traditional email-based patch review seamlessly.

It should also be possible to combine this with dispatch.sr.ht to have bidirectional code reviews between mailing lists and Github, Gitlab, etc - with no one on either side being any the wiser to the preferred workflow of the other.
Outgoing emails are edited with our embedded terminal emulator using vim, or your favorite $EDITOR.
I intend to sell this book, but when you buy it you'll receive a DRM-free CC-NC-ND copy that you can share freely with your friends.
I made a novel new Wayland compositor in my spare time which behaves like plan 9's Rio.
It's pretty clear by now that my productivity is way higher now that I'm able to work full-time on open source, thanks to your support.
key_insight: Bridging email-based and web-based development workflows can make both camps more productive without forcing either to abandon their preferred tools.
